---
title: "Qwen: Open-Weight Large Language and Multimodal Models"
author:
  - name: Claude

date: 2026-03-25
date-format: long
image: "../../../images/Qwen.png"

categories:
  - Models
  - Deep learning
  - NLP
  - Multimodal
  - Transformer
  - Language modeling
  - Text generation
---

Qwen (short for Tongyi Qianwen) is a family of open-weight large language and multimodal models developed by Alibaba Cloud. First released in 2023, the Qwen series has rapidly evolved through multiple generations — Qwen1.5, Qwen2, Qwen2.5, and QwQ — establishing itself as one of the most capable and accessible open-weight model families available. Qwen models span a wide range of sizes (0.5B to 72B+ parameters) and modalities (text, vision, audio, code, and math), making them suitable for tasks ranging from conversational AI and code generation to visual understanding and scientific reasoning.

#### Key features
- **Dense and Mixture-of-Experts Architectures**: Qwen offers both standard dense transformer models and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) variants that activate only a subset of parameters per token, enabling larger model capacity with efficient inference.
- **Multimodal Capabilities**: The Qwen family includes specialized models such as Qwen-VL (vision-language), Qwen-Audio, and Qwen2.5-Omni, supporting text, image, audio, and video understanding within a unified framework.
- **Long-Context Support**: Qwen2.5 models support context windows of up to 128K tokens, enabling processing of lengthy documents, codebases, and multi-turn conversations.
- **Multilingual Proficiency**: Trained on data spanning 29+ languages, Qwen models perform well across English, Chinese, and many other languages.
- **Permissive Licensing**: Most Qwen models are released under the Apache 2.0 license, allowing broad commercial and research use.

## Timeline context
Qwen fits into the rapidly evolving landscape of open-weight large language models. Here is a timeline placing Qwen in context with other important models.

- **[LLaMA (2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.13971)**: Meta's open-weight LLM that catalyzed the open-source LLM movement.
- **[Qwen (2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.16609)**: Alibaba Cloud's first-generation open-weight LLM family, competitive across benchmarks.
- **[Mistral 7B (2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06825)**: Efficient open-weight model with sliding window attention and grouped-query attention.
- **[LLaMA 2 (2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.09288)**: Improved open-weight models with RLHF-aligned chat variants.
- **[Qwen1.5 (2024)](https://qwenlm.github.io/blog/qwen1.5/)**: Refinement of the original Qwen series with improved alignment and multilingual support.
- **[LLaMA 3 (2024)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.21783)**: Meta's third-generation open-weight LLMs with expanded context and capabilities.
- **[Qwen2 (2024)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.10671)**: Major generational leap with improved reasoning, coding, and multilingual performance.
- **[Qwen2.5 (2024)](https://qwenlm.github.io/blog/qwen2.5/)**: State-of-the-art open-weight models with expanded sizes, MoE variants, and domain-specific models for code and math.
- **[QwQ (2024)](https://qwenlm.github.io/blog/qwq-32b-preview/)**: A reasoning-focused model leveraging chain-of-thought for complex problem solving.
- **[DeepSeek-R1 (2025)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.12948)**: Open-weight reasoning model advancing chain-of-thought capabilities.

## Qwen model families — what to use and when

The Qwen ecosystem covers a broad set of modalities and tasks. The table below summarizes the main model families and their intended use cases.

| Model Family | Modality | Sizes | Best For | Example Use Cases |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Qwen2.5** | Text | 0.5B – 72B | General-purpose language tasks | Chatbots, summarization, translation, Q&A |
| **Qwen2.5-Coder** | Text (code) | 1.5B – 32B | Code generation & understanding | Autocomplete, debugging, code review, docstrings |
| **Qwen2.5-Math** | Text (math) | 1.5B – 72B | Mathematical reasoning | Proofs, word problems, symbolic computation |
| **Qwen2.5-VL** | Text + Image/Video | 3B – 72B | Visual understanding | Document OCR, chart reading, visual Q&A |
| **Qwen2.5-Omni** | Text + Image + Audio + Video | 3B – 7B | Unified multimodal I/O | Voice assistants, video understanding, accessibility |
| **QwQ-32B** | Text (reasoning) | 32B | Complex multi-step reasoning | Research problems, math olympiad, agentic tasks |

### Choosing the right size
- **0.5B – 3B**: Edge devices, mobile, or latency-critical applications. Good for simple classification, extraction, and reformatting tasks.
- **7B – 14B**: The best balance of quality and cost for most research and production workloads. Strong general reasoning at manageable memory requirements.
- **32B – 72B**: When you need top-tier reasoning, nuanced instruction following, or state-of-the-art benchmark performance. Requires multi-GPU setups at full precision.

## How parameter count impacts GPU memory

Understanding memory requirements is critical when selecting a model size. The rule of thumb: **each billion parameters requires ~2 GB of VRAM in float16** (and ~1 GB in 8-bit quantization). The table below shows approximate VRAM needed to *load* Qwen2.5 models (inference only — training requires 2–3x more).

| Model | Parameters | float16 (FP16/BF16) | 8-bit (INT8) | 4-bit (GPTQ/AWQ) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Qwen2.5-0.5B | 0.5B | ~1 GB | ~0.5 GB | ~0.3 GB |
| Qwen2.5-1.5B | 1.5B | ~3 GB | ~1.5 GB | ~1 GB |
| Qwen2.5-3B | 3B | ~6 GB | ~3 GB | ~2 GB |
| Qwen2.5-7B | 7B | ~14 GB | ~7 GB | ~4 GB |
| Qwen2.5-14B | 14B | ~28 GB | ~14 GB | ~8 GB |
| Qwen2.5-32B | 32B | ~64 GB | ~32 GB | ~18 GB |
| Qwen2.5-72B | 72B | ~144 GB | ~72 GB | ~40 GB |

> **Note**: These are estimates for model weights only. Actual VRAM usage increases with context length (KV cache), batch size, and framework overhead. Long contexts (32K–128K tokens) can add several GB.

### Hands-on: Measure memory yourself

You can profile the actual GPU memory footprint of any Qwen model with the following snippet. Try swapping model sizes to see how parameter count directly impacts VRAM.

```python
# Requires: pip install transformers torch accelerate
import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

model_name = "Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct"  # Try: 0.5B, 1.5B, 3B, 7B, ...

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_name,
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,  # Change to torch.float32 to compare
    device_map="auto",          # Automatically uses GPU if available
)

# --- Check memory usage ---
if torch.cuda.is_available():
    mem_gb = torch.cuda.memory_allocated() / 1e9
    print(f"Model: {model_name}")
    print(f"GPU memory used: {mem_gb:.2f} GB")
    print(f"Parameters: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters()) / 1e9:.2f}B")
else:
    print("No GPU detected — model loaded on CPU.")
    print(f"Parameters: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters()) / 1e9:.2f}B")

# --- Run a quick generation to see peak memory ---
prompt = "Explain gradient descent in one paragraph."
inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to(model.device)

with torch.no_grad():
    output = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=100)

if torch.cuda.is_available():
    peak_gb = torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated() / 1e9
    print(f"Peak GPU memory during generation: {peak_gb:.2f} GB")

print("\nResponse:", tokenizer.decode(output[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
```

**Try these experiments:**

1. **Compare sizes**: Change `model_name` to `"Qwen/Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct"` or `"Qwen/Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct"` and compare the memory usage.
2. **Compare precisions**: Switch `torch_dtype` between `torch.float16` and `torch.float32` to see how precision halves/doubles memory.
3. **Try quantization**: Load a 4-bit quantized model to see drastic memory savings:

```python
# Requires: pip install transformers torch accelerate bitsandbytes
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, BitsAndBytesConfig

quantization_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(load_in_4bit=True)
model_4bit = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "Qwen/Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct",
    quantization_config=quantization_config,
    device_map="auto",
)

import torch
mem_gb = torch.cuda.memory_allocated() / 1e9
print(f"7B model in 4-bit: {mem_gb:.2f} GB")  # Expect ~4 GB vs ~14 GB in FP16
```

## Model playground
#### Tutorials and Getting Started
- **Qwen on Hugging Face**: Explore all Qwen models on the [Qwen Hugging Face collection](https://huggingface.co/Qwen). Each model card includes usage examples with the `transformers` library.
- **Official Documentation**: The [Qwen documentation](https://qwen.readthedocs.io/) covers installation, inference, fine-tuning, and deployment.
- **vLLM Deployment**: Qwen models are supported by [vLLM](https://docs.vllm.ai/) for high-throughput serving with efficient memory management.

#### High-level tips for effective use
- **Quantization**: For resource-constrained environments, use GPTQ or AWQ quantized versions (available on Hugging Face) to reduce memory usage with minimal quality loss.
- **Fine-tuning**: Use frameworks like [LLaMA-Factory](https://github.com/hiyouga/LLaMA-Factory) or Hugging Face TRL for supervised fine-tuning and RLHF on domain-specific data.
- **Prompt Engineering**: Qwen chat models follow a structured chat template. Use system prompts to guide behavior and formatting for best results.
- **Model Selection**: Start with smaller models (7B–14B) for rapid prototyping, then scale up to 32B–72B for production tasks requiring stronger reasoning.
- **Local Inference**: Use [Ollama](https://ollama.com/) or [llama.cpp](https://github.com/ggerganov/llama.cpp) with GGUF-format Qwen models for efficient local deployment.

#### Related benchmarks & challenges
- **[MMLU](https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.03300)**: A widely used benchmark for evaluating language understanding across 57 subjects. Qwen2.5-72B is highly competitive on this benchmark.
- **[HumanEval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2107.03374)**: A code generation benchmark where Qwen2.5-Coder models demonstrate strong performance.
- **[MATH](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.03874)**: A challenging mathematical reasoning benchmark where Qwen2.5-Math excels.

## Related resources
- [**Library**: Hugging Face](https://uw-madison-datascience.github.io/ML-X-Nexus/Toolbox/Libraries/HuggingFace.html): The primary platform for accessing, downloading, and fine-tuning Qwen models.
- [**Blog**: Qwen Technical Blog](https://qwenlm.github.io/blog/): Official blog with detailed technical reports and announcements for each Qwen release.
- [**Paper**: Qwen2.5 Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.10671): The technical report detailing architecture, training, and evaluation of the Qwen2 model family.

## Comments
